structured.summary: This paper proposes Language Model Self-Improvement (LMSI), a framework where large language models fine-tune themselves on their own high-confidence, self-generated Chain-of-Thought rationales and answers using unlabeled questions. LMSI achieves significant accuracy improvements across multiple reasoning benchmarks without ground truth supervision and demonstrates strong generalization and knowledge distillation to smaller models.

strengths.point: Novel self-improvement framework leveraging self-generated rationales without labeled data
strengths.evidence: LMSI fine-tunes LLMs on their own majority-voted Chain-of-Thought rationales and answers, enabling performance gains without any ground truth supervision.

strengths.point: Strong empirical performance and generalization across diverse reasoning tasks
strengths.evidence: LMSI improves PaLM-540B accuracy substantially on GSM8K, DROP, OpenBookQA, and ANLI-A3, achieving state-of-the-art or near state-of-the-art results and better out-of-domain generalization.

strengths.point: Comprehensive ablations and analysis of design choices
strengths.evidence: The paper studies the impact of mixed prompt formats, number of reasoning paths, sampling temperature, self-generated questions, and few-shot prompt generation.

strengths.point: Demonstrated knowledge distillation to smaller models
strengths.evidence: Smaller PaLM models (8B and 62B) distilled from LMSI-trained large models outperform same-sized models without LMSI.

weaknesses.point: Lack of controlled ablations isolating LMSI’s fine-tuning contribution from inference-time self-consistency
weaknesses.evidence: No direct comparison between baseline, self-consistency only, fine-tuning without self-consistency, and combined LMSI to clarify unique benefits of LMSI fine-tuning.

weaknesses.point: Insufficient analysis of label noise and majority vote reliability
weaknesses.evidence: No reported error rates of majority vote answers or experiments varying confidence thresholds to assess impact of noisy self-generated labels on fine-tuning.

weaknesses.point: Missing comparisons to alternative semi-supervised or pseudo-labeling baselines
weaknesses.evidence: No experiments comparing LMSI to standard semi-supervised learning methods using the same unlabeled data.

weaknesses.point: Limited evaluation of rationale quality and absence of human assessments
weaknesses.evidence: Evaluation focuses on accuracy without human or automatic evaluation of rationale correctness, coherence, or faithfulness.

weaknesses.point: Incomplete reproducibility details and prompt specifications
weaknesses.evidence: Fine-tuning hyperparameters, hardware details, exact sampling temperatures, and full prompt templates are not fully reported.

weaknesses.point: Potential confirmation bias by reinforcing model errors through self-labeling
weaknesses.evidence: Using majority vote answers from the model itself as pseudo-labels risks amplifying existing biases or mistakes without mitigation strategies.

weaknesses.point: Limited exploration of LMSI beyond PaLM models and tested reasoning domains
weaknesses.evidence: No evaluation on other architectures, smaller models beyond distillation, or broader reasoning tasks such as multi-hop QA or code generation.

weaknesses.point: No discussion of computational cost and efficiency considerations
weaknesses.evidence: Generating multiple reasoning paths and fine-tuning large models likely incurs high compute costs, which are not reported or analyzed.

questions.question: How much of LMSI’s performance gain comes specifically from fine-tuning on self-generated rationales versus inference-time self-consistency?
questions.motivation: Isolating this would clarify the unique contribution and novelty of the LMSI fine-tuning step.

questions.question: How robust is LMSI to noisy or incorrect majority vote labels, and can confidence thresholds or other filtering improve fine-tuning quality?
questions.motivation: Understanding and mitigating label noise is critical since LMSI relies solely on self-generated pseudo-labels without external verification.

critique_points.point_001: Lack of controlled ablations isolating LMSI’s fine-tuning contribution from inference-time self-consistency. No direct comparison between baseline, self-consistency only, fine-tuning without self-consistency, and combined LMSI to clarify unique benefits of LMSI fine-tuning
critique_points.point_002: Insufficient analysis of label noise and majority vote reliability. No reported error rates of majority vote answers or experiments varying confidence thresholds to assess impact of noisy self-generated labels on fine-tuning
critique_points.point_003: Missing comparisons to alternative semi-supervised or pseudo-labeling baselines. No experiments comparing LMSI to standard semi-supervised learning methods using the same unlabeled data
critique_points.point_004: Limited evaluation of rationale quality and absence of human assessments. Evaluation focuses on accuracy without human or automatic evaluation of rationale correctness, coherence, or faithfulness
critique_points.point_005: Incomplete reproducibility details and prompt specifications. Fine-tuning hyperparameters, hardware details, exact sampling temperatures, and full prompt templates are not fully reported
critique_points.point_006: Potential confirmation bias by reinforcing model errors through self-labeling. Using majority vote answers from the model itself as pseudo-labels risks amplifying existing biases or mistakes without mitigation strategies
critique_points.point_007: Limited exploration of LMSI beyond PaLM models and tested reasoning domains. No evaluation on other architectures, smaller models beyond distillation, or broader reasoning tasks such as multi-hop QA or code generation
critique_points.point_008: No discussion of computational cost and efficiency considerations. Generating multiple reasoning paths and fine-tuning large models likely incurs high compute costs, which are not reported or analyzed
